content.text: Shipping new software to a robot is not like deploying a web service. If a web service goes down, users see an error page. If a navigation stack rolls out a regression to a warehouse floor of 200 AMRs, robots collide, goods are damaged, and the facility may halt operations for hours. The discipline of sim-to-production operations is about building the gates, signals, and automated safeguards that let teams deploy with confidence — not by moving slowly, but by moving quickly with the ability to stop and reverse instantly.
What is it?

Sim-to-production operations (sim-to-prod) describes the full pipeline that takes a software change from a developer's workstation through simulation environments, staged hardware testing, and incremental live rollout to the full production robot fleet. It borrows heavily from web DevOps — continuous integration, GitOps, canary releases, feature flags — but applies them to a domain where the runtime environment is physical, failures are costly, and rollback must happen faster than a human can intervene.
The pipeline typically passes through four rings:
simulation
(Gazebo, Isaac Sim, or custom simulators running thousands of randomized scenarios),
hardware-in-the-loop staging
(real robot hardware running against simulated environments or real-world test tracks),
canary
(a small cohort of production robots, 5–10%, running the new software live), and
full production
(the remaining fleet, promoted after the canary cohort clears its soak period and SLO checks).
Why it matters

The cost asymmetry of robot failures drives the entire discipline. In simulation, a regression costs a few minutes of compute time. In staging, it costs a test run. In production, a navigation regression that causes a single collision can damage product worth thousands of dollars and trigger a safety review that halts operations for a day. A regression that affects charge docking accuracy across the fleet silently degrades throughput for days before it is detected. The gates between rings exist to catch regressions at the cheapest possible point.

A second driver is update frequency. Teams that release software to their robots once a month accumulate large batches of changes between releases, making root-cause analysis after a failure very difficult. Teams that release daily have smaller change sets, faster iteration, and faster root-cause isolation — but only if their release pipeline is fast and safe. Sim-to-prod is the infrastructure that makes high-frequency robot software deployment viable.
Benefits
Catches regressions at the simulation stage before they ever reach hardware, at near-zero marginal cost per test run
Shadow mode enables objective comparison of a new navigation policy against the production policy on identical real-world data before live deployment
Canary releases limit blast radius: a serious regression in production affects only 5–10% of the fleet before automated rollback fires
GitOps provides a complete, auditable deployment history — every version change is a git commit with a timestamp, author, and linked test results
OTA update automation eliminates the need to physically access robots for software updates, enabling fleet-scale releases in minutes
Automated SLO monitoring means regressions are detected and rollbacks initiated faster than any human operations team could respond
A/B testing of navigation policies on subsets of the fleet enables data-driven policy selection rather than opinion-based decisions
Trade-offs & limitations
Sim-to-real gap:
simulated environments do not perfectly replicate real-world physics, sensor noise, human behaviour, or hardware variation; failures that only occur on real hardware are invisible in simulation
Staging environment cost:
a full HIL (hardware-in-the-loop) staging environment requires dedicated robot hardware and test space, which is expensive and has limited parallelism
Canary selection bias:
a poorly selected canary cohort (e.g., all robots in a quiet area) may not expose a regression that only manifests in high-density traffic
Rollback complexity:
if the new software changes persistent state (e.g., updates a map format or a database schema), rolling back the software does not roll back the state change
Long soak periods:
intermittent failures require long canary soak times (24–72 hours) to appear with statistical significance, slowing release cadence
OTA reliability:
interrupted OTA updates (due to power loss or network failure) can leave robots in a partially-updated state; robust atomic install and rollback mechanisms are non-trivial to implement correctly
How it works

The pipeline is triggered by a commit to the main branch of the software repository. A CI system (GitHub Actions, Jenkins, Bazel) builds the software, runs unit and integration tests, and publishes a versioned artifact (an OCI image or a signed tar bundle) to the artifact registry. The SIL (Software-In-the-Loop) stage spins up the simulator with the new artifact and runs a regression suite — thousands of randomized scenarios — comparing navigation KPIs (collision rate, task success rate, path efficiency) against a baseline. If SIL passes, the artifact is promoted to the HIL (Hardware-In-the-Loop) stage.

HIL runs the new software on real robot hardware in a controlled test environment. The hardware exposes failure modes that simulation misses: real sensor noise profiles, actuator latency, firmware interactions, and environmental conditions (floor surface, lighting) that affect navigation. HIL test runs are slower (hours, not minutes) and fewer in number, but validate the real execution path before any production robot is touched.

After HIL, the artifact is tagged as a release candidate and deployed to the staging ring via GitOps: a commit updates the image tag in the staging overlay of the fleet-config repository, and Flux or ArgoCD reconciles the change, deploying it to the 5-robot staging cohort with full telemetry and shadow mode enabled. Shadow mode runs the new navigation policy alongside the current production policy on identical sensor data, comparing their outputs without the new policy actuating motors. After a soak period, shadow mode metrics are reviewed. If divergence rates and quality metrics pass threshold, the image tag is promoted to the canary overlay.
Architecture overview

attrs.alt: Sim to production pipeline showing four stages: Simulation (Gazebo/Isaac, SIL, HIL, CI gate), Staging (shadow mode, OTA to isolated fleet, A/B comparison), Canary (10% of fleet live, SLO monitoring, auto-rollback), and Production (100% fleet, immutable artifacts, rollback under 5 minutes), with promotion gates between stages and a telemetry feedback loop

content.text: Each stage has a promotion gate — a set of automated checks that must pass before the artifact advances. The gate between simulation and staging requires the SIL regression suite to pass at a defined threshold (e.g., task success rate > 99.8% across 10,000 scenarios). The gate between staging and canary requires shadow mode divergence below threshold and human sign-off from the safety team. The gate between canary and production requires the canary cohort to maintain SLOs (task success rate > 98%, zero safety-class incidents) for a defined soak period. The rollback arrow at the bottom reflects the automated signal: if the canary SLO is breached, Flux rolls back the canary overlay to the previous image tag within minutes.
When to use it
Any fleet operating more than ~5 robots where a concurrent navigation regression would have significant operational or safety impact
Navigation policy changes (new ML model, updated cost map parameters, changed velocity profiles) — these have high regression risk and benefit most from shadow mode evaluation
Firmware or OS updates on robot hardware — these interact with software in unexpected ways; HIL testing is especially important
Rapid release cadence (daily or weekly releases) — without automated gating, high-frequency releases increase regression risk to unacceptable levels
Mixed-vendor fleets where software updates to one robot type should not affect another type sharing the same facility
Safety-critical deployments (hospital, airport, public roads) where any unvalidated change to production is unacceptable
How to implement

Start with the CI pipeline and SIL stage. Set up your simulator to run headlessly on a CI runner, define a baseline KPI set (task success rate, collision rate, navigation efficiency), and write a test harness that generates randomized scenarios and compares new results against the baseline. This alone will catch most regressions before they reach hardware.

Add GitOps after you have CI working. Store your fleet configuration (robot group definitions, software image tags, navigation parameters) in a git repository with Kustomize overlays for each ring (staging, canary, production). Use Flux CD or ArgoCD to reconcile the desired state from the repository to your robot orchestration layer. Every deployment is now traceable to a git commit, and rollback is simply reverting the image tag commit.

# GitOps fleet deployment pipeline using Flux CD.
# Repository structure:
#
#   fleet-config/
#     base/
#       kustomization.yaml
#       nav-stack/
#         deployment.yaml   (robot navigation software container image)
#         config-map.yaml   (nav parameters, cost map config)
#     overlays/
#       staging/
#         kustomization.yaml  (5 robots, full telemetry, shadow mode on)
#       canary/
#         kustomization.yaml  (10% of prod robots)
#       production/
#         kustomization.yaml  (full fleet)
#
# Flux watches overlays/staging, overlays/canary, overlays/production.
# Promotion between rings is done by updating the image tag in the overlay
# and committing to the repo — Flux reconciles within 1 minute.

# overlays/staging/kustomization.yaml
---
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
  - ../../base
namespace: robot-staging

images:
  - name: ghcr.io/myorg/nav-stack
    newTag: v2.4.1-rc3   # candidate release under test

patches:
  - target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: nav-stack
    patch: |
      - op: replace
        path: /spec/replicas
        value: 5              # only 5 robots in staging ring
  - target:
      kind: ConfigMap
      name: nav-params
    patch: |
      - op: replace
        path: /data/shadow_mode
        value: "true"         # new model runs but doesn't actuate
      - op: replace
        path: /data/telemetry_level
        value: "full"         # capture everything for post-analysis
---
# overlays/canary/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
  - ../../base
namespace: robot-canary

images:
  - name: ghcr.io/myorg/nav-stack
    newTag: v2.4.1-rc3

patches:
  - target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: nav-stack
    patch: |
      - op: replace
        path: /spec/replicas
        value: 12             # 12 of 120 robots = 10%

configMapGenerator:
  - name: nav-params
    literals:
      - shadow_mode=false
      - telemetry_level=standard
      - canary_cohort=ring-2
---
# overlays/production/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
  - ../../base
namespace: robot-production

images:
  - name: ghcr.io/myorg/nav-stack
    newTag: v2.4.0           # currently promoted stable version
Examples
Shadow mode runs the new navigation policy on real sensor data without commanding motors, enabling direct comparison with the production policy:

"""
Shadow mode: run a new navigation policy alongside the production policy,
comparing their decisions without the new policy actually commanding motors.

Both policies receive identical sensor data. The shadow policy's outputs
are logged and compared to the production policy's outputs and real-world
outcomes to evaluate quality before live deployment.
"""

import time
import threading
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import logging

log = logging.getLogger("shadow_mode")


@dataclass
class NavigationCommand:
    linear_vel: float   # m/s
    angular_vel: float  # rad/s
    source: str         # "production" | "shadow"
    latency_ms: float


class ShadowModeEvaluator:
    """
    Runs production and shadow navigation policies side-by-side.
    Production commands go to motor controller; shadow commands are logged only.
    Records divergence metrics for offline analysis.
    """

    def __init__(self, production_policy, shadow_policy, motor_controller,
                 divergence_threshold: float = 0.3):
        self.prod = production_policy
        self.shadow = shadow_policy
        self.motors = motor_controller
        self.threshold = divergence_threshold
        self._divergence_count = 0
        self._total_steps = 0

    def step(self, sensor_data: dict) -> NavigationCommand:
        """
        Run both policies on the same sensor frame.
        Send production command to motors; log shadow command and divergence.
        """
        prod_cmd = self._run_policy(self.prod, sensor_data, "production")
        shadow_cmd = self._run_policy_async(self.shadow, sensor_data, "shadow")

        # Actuate — production policy only
        self.motors.send(prod_cmd.linear_vel, prod_cmd.angular_vel)

        self._total_steps += 1
        divergence = self._compute_divergence(prod_cmd, shadow_cmd)

        if divergence > self.threshold:
            self._divergence_count += 1
            log.warning(

Atomic OTA install with automatic rollback on health-check failure:

"""
OTA (Over-The-Air) update manager for a robot fleet.
Handles: signed bundle verification, atomic swap, rollback on failure.

Deployment model:
  - Software is packaged as an OCI image or a versioned tar.gz bundle.
  - Each robot fetches its bundle from the artifact registry.
  - An atomic swap installs the new version and restarts the nav stack.
  - If the new version fails its health check, the robot reverts to the
    previous version automatically within the rollback window.
"""

import hashlib
import subprocess
import shutil
import os
import json
import time
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

INSTALL_DIR = Path("/opt/robot/nav-stack")
BACKUP_DIR  = Path("/opt/robot/nav-stack-backup")
STATE_FILE  = Path("/opt/robot/ota-state.json")


def verify_bundle(bundle_path: Path, expected_sha256: str) -> bool:
    """Verify bundle integrity. Never install without verification."""
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(bundle_path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(65536), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    actual = h.hexdigest()
    if actual != expected_sha256:
        raise ValueError(f"SHA-256 mismatch: expected {expected_sha256}, got {actual}")
    return True


def atomic_install(bundle_path: Path, version: str) -> bool:
    """
    Install a new nav-stack version atomically:
    1. Backup current installation.
    2. Extract bundle to a temp directory.
    3. Rename (atomic on Linux) to the install directory.
    4. Write version state file.
    """
    temp_dir = INSTALL_DIR.parent / "nav-stack-temp"

    try:
        # Step 1: backup current
        if INSTALL_DIR.exists():
            if BACKUP_DIR.exists():
                shutil.rmtree(BACKUP_DIR)
            shutil.copytree(INSTALL_DIR, BACKUP_DIR)

        # Step 2: extract to temp
        if temp_dir.exists():
            shutil.rmtree(temp_dir)
        temp_dir.mkdir(parents=True)
        subprocess.run(["tar", "-xzf", str(bundle_path), "-C", str(temp_dir)],
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Real-world example

Waymo's deployment pipeline for its Driver software is the most documented in the autonomous vehicle industry. New model versions are tested in simulation across billions of virtual miles before any hardware evaluation. Hardware testing uses dedicated driver-assisted testing fleets. Shadow mode (called
"simulation in the cloud"

internally) runs new models against recordings of real-world drives, comparing predictions against ground truth without deploying to vehicles. Only models that pass all simulation, shadow, and staged ride-sharing evaluation gates are promoted to fully autonomous production deployment.

In warehouse robotics, Fetch Robotics (acquired by Zebra Technologies) operated a Continuous Deployment system for their AMR fleet software: new navigation stack versions were built by Jenkins, tested in a Gazebo simulation cluster, deployed to a 10-robot staging cluster in a test warehouse, and then rolled out to production fleets via a canary system that monitored task completion rates and automatically halted rollout if rates dropped. The key enabler was a REST API on each robot that accepted new software bundles and performed atomic swap with rollback — functionality they call "live update" in their SDK.
Common mistakes
Skipping the HIL stage to save time — sim-to-real gap means failures that only occur on real hardware are invisible until production canary; HIL is the cheapest place to catch them

Selecting the canary cohort from an atypical zone (e.g., low-traffic area near charging stations) — the cohort must represent the full diversity of operating conditions to catch regressions that only manifest under specific conditions
Using wall-clock time as the only soak criterion — certain failures are rare and need exposure to thousands of task executions, not just elapsed time; combine time soak with task count thresholds
Not versioning navigation parameters alongside code — a bug may be in a parameter value, not the binary; parameter changes need the same promotion pipeline as code changes
Treating simulation success as validation — simulation is a filter, not a proof; its value is catching obvious regressions cheaply, not guaranteeing correctness in all real-world conditions
Implementing OTA without atomic swap — a non-atomic install that fails midway leaves the robot in an inconsistent state that may not be recoverable without physical intervention
Failing to test the rollback path itself — rollback procedures are often written and never exercised until an emergency; run rollback drills in staging quarterly
Best practices
Treat every navigation parameter change as a code change — version it, gate it, and deploy it through the same pipeline as binary changes
Implement SLO-based automated rollback before deploying canary — a canary without automatic rollback is just a slower full-fleet deployment
Make the deployment state observable: a dashboard showing which software version is running on each robot, when it was deployed, and its current SLO status is essential for operations

Record shadow mode sessions for offline analysis — raw divergence rates are a leading indicator, but understanding why the new policy diverges requires reviewing the specific scenarios where it happened

Use immutable artifacts: once a version is built and tested, the binary should never be modified; if a config change is needed post-build, use a separate config management mechanism that the binary reads at startup

Implement a safe-stop command that can be broadcast to all robots instantly — in a worst-case scenario where the automated rollback is too slow, operators need a single command that stops all actuators across the entire fleet
Log software version, navigation parameters, and key sensor readings for every task execution — this data is the root-cause analysis foundation for any future incident
Performance & scalability

The SIL simulation stage scales horizontally: each test scenario is independent and can run on a separate CI worker. 10,000 scenarios that take 30 minutes on a single machine take 5 minutes on a 10-machine cluster. The bottleneck is usually scenario generation diversity rather than compute — ensuring the 10,000 scenarios cover the tail distributions of real-world operating conditions (unusual pedestrian trajectories, sensor noise profiles, timing edge cases) is harder than adding more machines.

OTA delivery at fleet scale (500+ robots) requires a content delivery infrastructure. Pushing a 2 GB software bundle to 500 robots over a single NAS link is impractical. Production systems use a peer-assisted or CDN-like delivery model: a small number of robots in each zone receive the bundle first, then serve it to nearby robots over the local network, dramatically reducing the load on the central artifact server. Bandwidth scheduling (rolling the update out over 4 hours during low-traffic periods rather than all at once) further protects operational continuity.
Tooling & ecosystem
Tool
Purpose
Notes
Flux CD / ArgoCD
GitOps continuous delivery for robot software
Flux v2 preferred; uses Kustomize or Helm overlays per ring
Gazebo / NVIDIA Isaac Sim
SIL simulation and scenario generation
Isaac Sim offers better physics fidelity; Gazebo has wider ecosystem
Bazel / Buck
Hermetic, reproducible robot software builds
Ensures bit-for-bit identical artifacts across CI and staging
Prometheus + Grafana
SLO monitoring and alerting for canary cohorts
Alert on task_success_rate drop > 1% within 30-minute window
Backstage / internal portal
Developer visibility into deployment rings and artifact status
Shows which version is in each ring and its gate status
OCI registries (GHCR, ECR)
Versioned artifact storage for robot software bundles
Sign with cosign (Sigstore); verify on robot before install

Security & reliability

OTA update channels are a high-value attack surface. An attacker who can inject a malicious software bundle into the update pipeline has effectively rooted the entire fleet. Mitigations: (1) sign all artifacts with a private key (Sigstore/cosign is the current standard); (2) verify signatures on the robot before unpacking any bundle; (3) use mutual TLS for all artifact downloads; (4) implement a hardware root of trust (TPM or secure enclave) on robots that validates the boot chain and software signatures before allowing the robot to operate.

Reliability of the deployment system itself matters as much as reliability of the deployed software. If the GitOps controller goes down, robots must continue operating on their current software version — not fail-safe to a stopped state. Design the fleet for independence from the deployment infrastructure: robots boot from local storage, operate autonomously between updates, and request updates on their own schedule rather than being pushed updates imperatively. This also makes the update system resilient to partial network outages.
Key takeaways

The sim-to-production pipeline has four rings (simulation, staging/HIL, canary, production) with automated promotion gates between them; failures caught earlier in the pipeline cost orders of magnitude less than failures caught later
Shadow mode runs the new policy on real sensor data without actuating motors, enabling head-to-head comparison against the production policy before any live deployment risk is incurred
GitOps (Flux CD or ArgoCD) manages fleet configuration as code: every deployment is a git commit, rollback is a git revert, and the desired state is always auditable
Canary releases deploy to 5–10% of the fleet; automated SLO monitoring (task success rate, collision rate) triggers rollback without human intervention if the cohort's performance degrades

OTA updates must be atomic with rollback: verify the signed bundle, install to a temp directory, rename atomically, run a health check, and revert to the backup if the health check fails — never install in-place without a fallback

The sim-to-real gap means simulation catches the majority of regressions but not all; HIL testing on real hardware in controlled conditions is the essential bridge between perfect simulation and unpredictable production
Security of the OTA channel is as important as security of the robot itself — sign artifacts with a hardware-backed key, verify on device, and audit every OTA event
